# Toxicological Concerns of Engineered Nanosize Drug Delivery Systems

### Affiliation

# Toxicological Concerns of Engineered Nanosize Drug Delivery Systems

### Authors

### Affiliation

## Abstract

Matters when converted into nanosize provide some unique surface properties, which are different from those of the bulk materials. Nanomaterials show some extraordinary behavioral patterns because of those properties, such as supermagnetism, quantum confinement, etc. A great deal of implication of nanomaterials in nanomedicine has already been realized. Utility of nanomaterials as drug nanocarrier projects many potential advantages of them in drug delivery. Despite many such advantages, the potential risk of health and environmental hazards related to them cannot be ignored. Here various physicochemical factors, such as chemical nature, degradability, surface properties, surface charge, particle size, and shape, have been shown to play a crucial role in toxicity related to drug nanocarriers. Evidence-based findings of some drug nanocarriers have been incorporated to provide distinct knowledge to the readers in the field. A glimpse of current regulatory controls and measures required to combat the challenges of toxicological aspects of drug nanocarriers have been described.

# Nanotechnologies

#### 6. What are potential harmful effects of nanoparticles?

#### 6.1 Can nanoparticles interact with living organisms?

[Nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm), can have the same dimensions as [biological molecules](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/abc/biomolecule.htm) such as [proteins](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/pqrs/protein.htm).

In living systems, they may immediately [adsorb](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/abc/adsorb-adsorption.htm) onto their surface some of the large [molecules](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/molecule.htm) they encounter as they enter the [tissues](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/tuv/tissue.htm) and fluids of the body.

This ability of [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) to have [molecules](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/molecule.htm) “sticking” to their surface depends on the surface characteristics of the particles and can be relevant for drug delivery uses. Indeed, it is possible to deliver a drug directly to a specific [cell](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/abc/cell.htm) in the body by designing the surface of a nanoparticle so that it [adsorbs](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/abc/adsorb-adsorption.htm) specifically onto the surface of the target cell.

But the interaction with living systems is also affected by the dimensions of the [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm). For instance, nanoparticles no bigger than a few [nanometres](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanometre.htm) may reach well inside [biomolecules](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/abc/biomolecule.htm), which is not possible for larger nanoparticles. Nanoparticles may cross [cell membranes](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/abc/cell-membrane.htm). It has been reported that [inhaled](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/ghi/inhalation-inhale.htm) nanoparticles can reach the blood and may reach other target sites such as the liver, heart or blood cells.

Key factors in the interaction with living structures include nanoparticle [dose](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/def/dose-non-radioactive.htm), the ability of [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) to spread within the body, as well as their [solubility](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/pqrs/soluble-solubility.htm). Some nanoparticles dissolve easily and their effects on living organisms are the same as the effects of the chemical they are made of. However, other nanoparticles do not degrade or dissolve readily. Instead, they may [accumulate](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/abc/bioaccumulation-bioaccumulate.htm) in biological systems and persist for a long time, which makes such nanoparticles of particular concern.

There remain many unknown details about the interaction of [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) and biological systems and more information on the response of living organisms to the presence of nanoparticles of varying size, shape, chemical composition and surface characteristics is needed to understand and categorize the [toxicity](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/tuv/toxicity.htm) of nanoparticles.
[More...](../l-3/6-health-effects-nanoparticles.htm#1p0)

#### 6.2 Which characteristics of nanoparticles are relevant for health effects?

Studies specifically dealing with the [toxicity](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/tuv/toxicity.htm) of [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) have only appeared recently and are still scarce. Most of the information available comes from studies on [inhaled](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/ghi/inhalation-inhale.htm) nanoparticles and from pharmaceutical studies in which [nanomaterials](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanomaterial.htm) are used, among other things to improve drug delivery.

The characteristics of [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) that are relevant for health effects are:

[More...](../l-3/6-health-effects-nanoparticles.htm#2p0)

#### 6.3 How can inhaled nanoparticles affect health?

[Particulate matter](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/pqrs/particulate-matter.htm) present in air pollution, especially from traffic emissions, is known to affect human health, although it is not clear exactly how. [Epidemiological studies](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/def/epidemiological-studies.htm) on [ambient](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/abc/ambient.htm) air pollution have not proved conclusively that [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) are more harmful than larger particles, but these studies may not be well suited to demonstrate such differences.

[Inhaled](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/ghi/inhalation-inhale.htm) [particulate matter](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/pqrs/particulate-matter.htm) can be deposited throughout the human [respiratory tract](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/pqrs/respiratory-tract.htm), and an important fraction of inhaled [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) deposit in the lungs. Nanoparticles can potentially move from the lungs to other organs such as the brain, the liver, the [spleen](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/pqrs/spleen.htm) and possibly the [foetus](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/def/fetus.htm) in pregnant women. Data on these pathways is extremely limited but the actual number of particles that move from one organ to another can be considerable, depending on [exposure](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/def/exposure-exposed-expose.htm) time. Even within the [nanoscale](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoscale.htm), size is important and small nanoparticles have been shown to be more able to reach secondary organs than larger ones.

Another potential route of [inhaled](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/ghi/inhalation-inhale.htm) [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) within the body is the [olfactory nerve](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/olfactory-nerve.htm); nanoparticles may cross the [mucous membrane](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/mucous-membrane.htm) inside the nose and then reach the brain through the olfactory nerve. Out of three human studies, only one showed a passage of inhaled nanoparticles into the bloodstream.

Materials which by themselves are not very harmful could be [toxic](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/tuv/toxic.htm) if they are [inhaled](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/ghi/inhalation-inhale.htm) in the form of [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm).

The effects of [inhaled](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/ghi/inhalation-inhale.htm) [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) in the body may include lung [inflammation](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/ghi/inflammation.htm) and heart problems. Studies in humans show that breathing in diesel soot causes a general [inflammatory response](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/ghi/inflammation.htm) and alters the system that regulates the involuntary functions in the [cardiovascular system](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/abc/cardiovascular-system-circulatory-system.htm), such as control of heart rate.

The [pulmonary](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/pqrs/pulmonary.htm) injury and [inflammation](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/ghi/inflammation.htm) resulting from the [inhalation](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/ghi/inhalation-inhale.htm) of [nanosize](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoscale.htm) urban [particulate matter](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/pqrs/particulate-matter.htm) appears to be due to the [oxidative stress](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/oxidative-stress.htm) that these particles cause in the cells.
[More...](../l-3/6-health-effects-nanoparticles.htm#3p0)

#### 6.4 What are the health implications of nanoparticles used as drug carriers?

[Nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) can be used for drug delivery purposes, either as the drug itself or as the drug carrier. The product can be administered orally, applied onto the skin, or injected.

The objective of drug delivery with [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) is either to get more of the drug to the target cells or to reduce the harmful effects of the free drug on other organs, or both. Nanoparticles used in this way have to circulate long distances evading the protection mechanisms of the body. To achieve this, nanoparticles are conceived to stick to [cell membranes](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/abc/cell-membrane.htm), get inside specific cells in the body or in [tumours](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/tuv/tumour-tumor.htm), and pass through cells. The surfaces of nanoparticles are sometimes also modified to avoid being recognized and eliminated by the [immune system](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/ghi/immune-system.htm).

With dermal administration, it was found that particle size was less important than the total [charge](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/abc/charge.htm) in terms of permeation through the skin. For instance, only negatively charged particles were found to overcome the skin barrier and only when concentration of charge was high enough.

[Nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) may be used effectively to deliver [genes](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/ghi/gene.htm) to cells, to treat [cancer](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/abc/cancer.htm), as well as in vaccination .

The use of [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) as drug carriers may reduce the [toxicity](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/tuv/toxicity.htm) of the incorporated drug but it is sometimes difficult to distinguish the toxicity of the drug from that of the nanoparticle. Toxicity of gold nanoparticles, for instance, has been shown at high concentrations. In addition, nanoparticles trapped in the liver can affect the function of this organ.

[Nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) have the potential to cross the [blood brain barrier](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/abc/blood-brain-barrier.htm), which makes them extremely useful as a way to deliver drugs directly to the brain. On the other hand, this is also a major drawback because nanoparticles used to carry drugs may be [toxic](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/tuv/toxic.htm) to the brain.
[More...](../l-3/6-health-effects-nanoparticles.htm#4p0)

#### 6.5 How should harmful effects of nanoparticles be assessed?

Traditionally, [doses](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/def/dose-non-radioactive.htm) are measured in terms of mass because the harmful effects of any substance depend on the mass of the substance to which the individual is [exposed](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/def/exposure-exposed-expose.htm). However, for [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) it is more reasonable to measure doses also in terms of number of particles and their surface area because these parameters further determine the interactions of nanoparticles with biological systems.

Several hypotheses were proposed for the adverse health effects of [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) as part of [ambient](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/abc/ambient.htm) air pollution. These hypotheses address nanoparticle characteristics, their distribution, and their effects on organ systems, including effects on [immune](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/ghi/immune-system.htm) and [inflammatory](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/ghi/inflammation.htm) systems.

However, some of these hypotheses may be of limited or no relevance for engineered [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm). For instance, the adhesion of [toxic](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/tuv/toxic.htm) substances onto the surface of nanoparticles may be of less relevance for production and handling facilities of large volumes of engineered nanoparticles compared to the particles in [ambient](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/abc/ambient.htm) air.

In addition, drawing conclusions from tests on healthy animal models may be unsuitable as some of the effects of [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) may only be a risk for susceptible organisms and predisposed individuals, but not to healthy people. For instance, age, [respiratory tract](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/pqrs/respiratory-tract.htm) problems and other pollutants can modify the [pulmonary](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/pqrs/pulmonary.htm) [inflammation](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/ghi/inflammation.htm) and [oxidative stress](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/oxidative-stress.htm) induced by nanoparticles.

Because of the specific characteristics of [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm), conventional [toxicity](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/tuv/toxicity.htm) tests may not be enough to detect all their possible harmful effects. Therefore, a series of specific tests was proposed to assess the toxicity of nanoparticles used in drug delivery systems. One mechanism of toxicity of nanoparticles is likely to be the induction of [oxidative stress](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/oxidative-stress.htm) in cells and organs. Testing for interaction of nanoparticles with [proteins](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/pqrs/protein.htm) and various [cell](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/abc/cell.htm) types should be considered as part of the toxicological evaluation.

With the exception of airborne particles delivered to the lung, information on the behaviour of [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) in the body including distribution, [accumulation](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/abc/bioaccumulation-bioaccumulate.htm), [metabolism](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/metabolism.htm), and organ specific [toxicity](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/tuv/toxicity.htm) is still minimal.
[More...](../l-3/6-health-effects-nanoparticles.htm#5p0)

#### 6.6 What are the effects of nanoparticles on the environment?

There are almost no publications on the effects of engineered [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) on animals and plants in the environment.

However, a number of studies have examined the uptake and effects of [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) at a [cellular](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/abc/cell.htm) level to evaluate their impact on humans; it can reasonably be assumed that the conclusions of these studies may be extrapolated to other species, but more research is needed to confirm this assumption. Moreover, careful examination and interpretation of existing data and careful planning of new research is required to establish the true impact of nanoparticles on the environment, and the differences with larger, conventional forms of the substances.

Persistent insoluble [nanoparticles](https://ec.europa.eu/health/scientific_committees/opinions_layman/glossary/mno/nanoparticle.htm) may cause problems in the environment that are much greater than those revealed by human health assessments.
[More...](../l-3/6-health-effects-nanoparticles.htm#6p0)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Nanoparticles may induce harmful physiological responses, such as inflammation or toxicity, impacting organ function, as seen with drug-loaded nanoparticles that enhance toxicity in cardiac tissues.
Nanoparticles' unique properties allow them to interact with biological molecules, potentially leading to unintended effects, including cellular uptake and disruption of normal cellular functions.

The persistence of nanoparticles in biological systems raises concerns about accumulation and long-term toxicity, necessitating comprehensive studies to assess chronic exposure effects on human health.
